Compute-Aware Mixture-of-Experts for 5G Neural Receivers¶

Progress Defense — 2026-04-30¶

Team: Dominik Huml (xhumld00), Jakub Kontrík (xkontr02), Martin Vaculík (xvaculm00)

Repository: https://github.com/TheRamsay/moe-5g-nrx

In one sentence¶

We built a 5G neural receiver where a small router examines channel quality features and dispatches each received OFDM slot to one of three different-sized experts — matching the dense baseline's BLER while using only 56% of the FLOPs, with the routing learned from a FLOPs penalty in the loss (no SNR labels).

What's new since the checkpoint report¶

The checkpoint report ended at: asymmetric warm-start, 0.910 BLER, 61% FLOPs, single seed. Since then we have run roughly ten new experiments. Here is the complete summary; each is detailed below.

# Experiment Status Key finding
1 Alpha sweep (4 values) ✅ exp26 (α=2e-3) is the Pareto knee: 0.902 BLER / 56% FLOPs
2 3-seed confirmation ✅ Bimodal: 2/3 reproduce (s67, s42), 1/3 collapses (s32 → 0.958)
3 Stabilization ×2 ⚠️ large-warmup → 100% large; β-warmup → worse BLER. Both failed.
4 Random-router ablation ✅ Replace router input with noise → BLER craters 6.6 pp
5 2-expert ablation ✅ Dropping nano costs 0.7 pp BLER + 9 pp FLOPs
6 DeepMIMO OOD eval ⚠️ All models fail (~0.99 BLER) on ray-traced data
7 Few-shot fine-tune ⚠️ 500 steps / 2k samples insufficient. Honest scope.
8 SNR-oracle baseline ✅ Hand-rule with true SNR: 0.900 / 49%. exp26 on Pareto without oracle.
9 Explicit SNR-input ablation (exp38, today) ✅ Adding SNR proxies → collapse to 100% large

Legend: ✅ = positive result that informs design; ⚠️ = honest negative result.

In [1]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import Image, Markdown, display
from pathlib import Path

FIGURES = Path('figures')

# Visual style
plt.rcParams.update({
    'figure.figsize': (10, 5.5),
    'axes.grid': True,
    'grid.alpha': 0.25,
    'axes.spines.top': False,
    'axes.spines.right': False,
    'font.size': 11,
    'axes.titlesize': 13,
    'axes.titleweight': 'bold',
    'axes.labelsize': 11,
    'legend.frameon': False,
})

# Consistent color palette
C = {
    'large': '#1f4e79',  # deep blue
    'small': '#5b9bd5',  # mid blue
    'nano': '#a8d4f0',   # light blue
    'exp26': '#c0504d',  # red — our winner
    'oracle': '#9d9d9d', # grey — reference
    'fail': '#cf6f6f',   # muted red — negative result
    'ok': '#4a7c59',     # green — positive result
}

def fig_exists(name):
    return (FIGURES / name).exists()

print('Setup complete.')

Setup complete.

1. The problem¶

A 5G neural receiver decodes OFDM symbols into bits. The dense baseline (Wiesmayr et al. 2024) applies the same compute to every slot regardless of how easy or hard it is.

But channel difficulty varies enormously across slots:

  • Easy slots: high SNR, line-of-sight, slow fading → could be decoded by a tiny network
  • Hard slots: waterfall region (5–10 dB SNR), deep fading, high mobility → need the full receiver

Static dense receivers waste compute on the easy ones. We want compute that scales with channel difficulty.

Why it matters: mobile devices have limited battery and thermal budgets. Adaptive compute saves energy.

2. Architecture¶

Three components in series:

  1. Shared stem (285M FLOPs, always paid) — processes received signal + LS channel estimate, produces features
  2. Channel-aware router (tiny MLP) — pools stem features (mean+max over freq, time), outputs probability over 3 experts
  3. Three heterogeneous experts — different compute capacities, only ONE runs at inference (hard top-1)

Loss: $L = L_{BCE} + \gamma \cdot L_{channel\_MSE} + \alpha \cdot \mathbb{E}[\text{FLOPs ratio}] + \beta \cdot L_{balance}$

  • $\gamma = 0.05$ (channel estimation regularizer — domain-specific)
  • $\alpha$ controls FLOPs/quality tradeoff (we sweep this)
  • $\beta = 0.1$ prevents collapse to a single expert
In [2]:
# Show architecture if available
if fig_exists('archictures.png'):
    display(Image(filename=str(FIGURES / 'archictures.png'), width=900))
else:
    print('Architecture figure not found locally.')
In [3]:
# Expert sizes — derived from pre-trained dense baselines
experts = pd.DataFrame({
    'Expert': ['nano', 'small', 'large'],
    'Block dim': [8, 32, 64],
    'Blocks': [4, 8, 8],
    'Params (k)': [90, 168, 450],
    'Total FLOPs (M)': [320, 695, 1604],
    '% of large': ['20%', '43%', '100%'],
})
experts.style.set_caption('Heterogeneous experts (sized to match pre-trained dense baselines)')
Out[3]:
Heterogeneous experts (sized to match pre-trained dense baselines)
  Expert Block dim Blocks Params (k) Total FLOPs (M) % of large
0 nano 8 4 90 320 20%
1 small 32 8 168 695 43%
2 large 64 8 450 1604 100%

3. The training journey — three phases, two failure modes, one fix¶

We hit two opposite failure modes before finding what works.

Phase 1 — joint training from scratch (cold start)¶

All experts random init. The FLOPs penalty kicks in early when none of them are good. Router learns: "large is expensive, abandon it."

→ BLER 0.926 / 48% FLOPs — cheap but BLER bleeds.

Phase 2 — full warm-start¶

Each expert initialized from its pre-trained dense checkpoint. Router sees: "warm-large is strictly better than warm-nano/small from step 1." Locks onto large, never explores.

→ BLER 0.879 / 100% FLOPs — basically a fine-tuned dense large.

Anti-collapse experiments (5 attempts, all failed)¶

Stronger β, β=2.0 forced uniform, capacity constraints, Switch-Transformer auxiliary loss — all either collapse or kill BLER.

Phase 3 — asymmetric warm-start (the breakthrough)¶

Warm-start stem + nano + small from dense checkpoints, but leave large at random init. Large has no initial advantage; the router uses only nano/small for ~6–8k steps. Then large "wakes up" once it trains up, and the router discovers it.

→ All three experts active by step 10–12k. This is the recipe everything else is built on.

In [4]:
# Visualize the three phases
phases = pd.DataFrame({
    'Phase': ['Phase 1\n(cold start)', 'Phase 2\n(full warm)', 'Phase 3\n(asym warm) ⭐'],
    'BLER': [0.926, 0.879, 0.902],
    'FLOPs %': [48, 100, 56],
})

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

colors = [C['fail'], C['fail'], C['ok']]
ax1.bar(phases['Phase'], phases['BLER'], color=colors, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax1.axhline(y=0.901, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label='Dense large baseline (0.901)')
ax1.set_ylabel('Average BLER (lower is better)')
ax1.set_title('BLER across three training approaches')
ax1.set_ylim(0.85, 0.95)
ax1.legend(loc='upper right')
for i, v in enumerate(phases['BLER']):
    ax1.text(i, v + 0.002, f'{v:.3f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

ax2.bar(phases['Phase'], phases['FLOPs %'], color=colors, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.8)
ax2.axhline(y=100, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, label='Dense large = 100%')
ax2.set_ylabel('Average FLOPs (% of dense large)')
ax2.set_title('FLOPs across three training approaches')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 120)
ax2.legend(loc='upper right')
for i, v in enumerate(phases['FLOPs %']):
    ax2.text(i, v + 2, f'{v}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('Phase 1 and Phase 2 fail in OPPOSITE directions. Asym warm-start is the only fix.', 
             fontsize=12, y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Show training dynamics of the asym warm-start (when large 'wakes up')
if fig_exists('expert_usage_asym_warm.png'):
    print('Expert usage during asymmetric warm-start training:')
    display(Image(filename=str(FIGURES / 'expert_usage_asym_warm.png'), width=850))
    print('\nLarge (random init) crashes to ~0% by step 2000, then begins receiving traffic at step ~6500')
    print('and reaches competitive usage by step ~8000-10000. The router *discovers* large once it earns its spot.')

Expert usage during asymmetric warm-start training:

Large (random init) crashes to ~0% by step 2000, then begins receiving traffic at step ~6500
and reaches competitive usage by step ~8000-10000. The router *discovers* large once it earns its spot.

4. The headline result — alpha sweep finds the Pareto knee¶

After asymmetric warm-start worked, we ran a 4-point sweep over the FLOPs penalty α to find the best operating point on the BLER vs FLOPs Pareto curve.

In [6]:
# Alpha sweep results (test split, 50k subset)
sweep = pd.DataFrame({
    'Run': ['Dense large', 'exp24', 'exp25', 'exp26 ⭐', 'exp27'],
    'α': ['—', '5e-4', '1e-3', '2e-3', '5e-3'],
    'Avg BLER': [0.901, 0.898, 0.907, 0.902, 0.911],
    'FLOPs %': [100, 100, 56, 56, 60],
    'Routing l/n/s': ['—', '100/0/0', '44/12/44', '44/15/40', '37/0/63'],
    'Verdict': ['reference', 'α too weak → collapse to large', 'dominated by exp26',
                'KNEE OF PARETO', 'α too strong → nano starved'],
})

def style_sweep(s):
    color = ['background-color: #fff4d6' if r == 'exp26 ⭐' else '' for r in s]
    return color

sweep.style.apply(style_sweep, subset=['Run']).set_caption('Alpha sweep: exp26 dominates the rest')
Out[6]:
Alpha sweep: exp26 dominates the rest
  Run α Avg BLER FLOPs % Routing l/n/s Verdict
0 Dense large — 0.901000 100 — reference
1 exp24 5e-4 0.898000 100 100/0/0 α too weak → collapse to large
2 exp25 1e-3 0.907000 56 44/12/44 dominated by exp26
3 exp26 ⭐ 2e-3 0.902000 56 44/15/40 KNEE OF PARETO
4 exp27 5e-3 0.911000 60 37/0/63 α too strong → nano starved
In [7]:
# Pareto plot — clean layout with no label collisions
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))

# Dense baselines (square markers, blue)
dense = [
    {'name': 'dense_nano',  'BLER': 0.966, 'FLOPs': 20,  'offset': (10, 6)},
    {'name': 'dense_small', 'BLER': 0.931, 'FLOPs': 43,  'offset': (10, 6)},
    {'name': 'dense_large', 'BLER': 0.901, 'FLOPs': 100, 'offset': (-50, 12)},
]
for d in dense:
    ax.scatter(d['FLOPs'], d['BLER'], s=240, marker='s', color=C['large'],
               edgecolor='black', linewidth=1.3, zorder=5,
               label='Dense baseline' if d['name'] == 'dense_nano' else None)
    ax.annotate(d['name'], (d['FLOPs'], d['BLER']),
                textcoords='offset points', xytext=d['offset'],
                fontsize=10, color=C['large'], fontweight='bold')

# MoE alpha sweep (circles)
moe = [
    {'name': 'exp24',  'alpha': 'α=5e-4', 'BLER': 0.898, 'FLOPs': 100, 'offset': (12, -2),  'highlight': False},
    {'name': 'exp25',  'alpha': 'α=1e-3', 'BLER': 0.907, 'FLOPs': 56,  'offset': (-65, 10), 'highlight': False},
    {'name': 'exp26',  'alpha': 'α=2e-3', 'BLER': 0.902, 'FLOPs': 56,  'offset': (12, -2),  'highlight': True},
    {'name': 'exp27',  'alpha': 'α=5e-3', 'BLER': 0.911, 'FLOPs': 60,  'offset': (12, 6),   'highlight': False},
]
labelled = False
for m in moe:
    if m['highlight']:
        ax.scatter(m['FLOPs'], m['BLER'], s=420, color=C['exp26'],
                   edgecolor='black', linewidth=2.5, zorder=7,
                   marker='*', label='exp26 (winner)')
    else:
        kw = {'label': 'MoE alpha sweep'} if not labelled else {}
        ax.scatter(m['FLOPs'], m['BLER'], s=180, color='#4a7c59',
                   edgecolor='black', linewidth=1.0, zorder=6, **kw)
        labelled = True
    label_text = f"{m['name']}\n({m['alpha']})"
    fw = 'bold' if m['highlight'] else 'normal'
    fs = 10.5 if m['highlight'] else 9.5
    color = C['exp26'] if m['highlight'] else 'black'
    ax.annotate(label_text, (m['FLOPs'], m['BLER']),
                textcoords='offset points', xytext=m['offset'],
                fontsize=fs, fontweight=fw, color=color, ha='left')

# Pareto frontier line — between exp26 (best at 56%) and exp24/dense_large (best at 100%)
pareto_x = [56, 100]
pareto_y = [0.902, 0.898]
ax.plot(pareto_x, pareto_y, '--', color=C['exp26'], alpha=0.55, linewidth=2.0,
        label='Pareto frontier', zorder=2)

# Highlight the "matches dense" zone
ax.axhspan(0.898, 0.904, alpha=0.10, color=C['exp26'], zorder=1)

# Annotate the key insight
ax.annotate('exp26: same BLER as dense_large\nat ~half the compute',
            xy=(56, 0.902), xytext=(70, 0.918),
            fontsize=11, color=C['exp26'], fontweight='bold',
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=C['exp26'], lw=1.5),
            bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.4', facecolor='white',
                      edgecolor=C['exp26'], linewidth=1.2))

ax.set_xlabel('Average FLOPs (% of dense large)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Average BLER (lower is better)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('BLER vs FLOPs — Pareto frontier of the alpha sweep',
             fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlim(10, 115)
ax.set_ylim(0.885, 0.98)
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=10.5, framealpha=0.95)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print('\nKey result: exp26 (α=2e-3) matches dense_large quality (0.902 vs 0.901 BLER)')
print('using only 56% FLOPs. Strict Pareto improvement over the asym-warm anchor (0.910 / 61%).')

Key result: exp26 (α=2e-3) matches dense_large quality (0.902 vs 0.901 BLER)
using only 56% FLOPs. Strict Pareto improvement over the asym-warm anchor (0.910 / 61%).

5. Ablations — proving the design choices are load-bearing¶

These are the questions a sharp reviewer would ask. We answered all three.

5.1 Is the router actually using channel features, or could it be random?¶

We replaced the router input with torch.randn — same training recipe, same losses, same architecture, but the router can no longer see channel quality.

Result: BLER craters by 6.6 pp AND the router collapses to small (large is never used).

This is the central claim of the project: channel-aware features matter causally.

In [8]:
# Random router ablation
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

labels = ['exp26\n(channel-aware)', 'exp30\n(random router)']
bler = [0.902, 0.968]
colors_a = [C['ok'], C['fail']]

bars = ax1.bar(labels, bler, color=colors_a, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax1.axhline(y=0.901, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Dense large')
ax1.set_ylabel('Average BLER')
ax1.set_title('BLER — random input destroys quality (+6.6 pp)')
ax1.set_ylim(0.85, 1.0)
for i, v in enumerate(bler):
    ax1.text(i, v + 0.005, f'{v:.3f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
ax1.legend()

# Routing distribution
routing_data = {
    'large': [44, 0],
    'nano': [15, 11],
    'small': [40, 89],
}
x = np.arange(2)
width = 0.6
bottoms = np.zeros(2)
for expert, vals in routing_data.items():
    ax2.bar(x, vals, width, bottom=bottoms, label=expert,
            color=C[expert], edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.7)
    for i, v in enumerate(vals):
        if v > 5:
            ax2.text(x[i], bottoms[i] + v / 2, f'{v}%', ha='center', va='center',
                     color='white' if expert == 'large' else 'black', fontweight='bold')
    bottoms += np.array(vals)

ax2.set_xticks(x)
ax2.set_xticklabels(labels)
ax2.set_ylabel('Routing distribution (%)')
ax2.set_title('Routing — random input collapses to small (no large used)')
ax2.legend(loc='upper right')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 110)

plt.suptitle('ABLATION: Channel-aware features are load-bearing (the central project claim)',
             fontsize=12, y=1.02, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5.2 Do you really need three experts, or would two suffice?¶

We dropped nano and trained with only {small, large}.

Result: 0.7 pp worse BLER + 9 pp more FLOPs.

Nano is non-decorative — it absorbs hopeless low-SNR samples that small would burn compute on. Without nano, small picks up that traffic instead of large.

In [9]:
# 2-expert ablation
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

labels = ['exp26\n(3 experts)', 'exp31\n(2 experts, no nano)']
bler = [0.902, 0.909]
flops = [56, 65]

ax1.bar(labels, bler, color=[C['ok'], C['fail']], edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax1.axhline(y=0.901, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Dense large')
ax1.set_ylabel('Average BLER')
ax1.set_title('BLER (2-expert is 0.7 pp worse)')
ax1.set_ylim(0.88, 0.93)
for i, v in enumerate(bler):
    ax1.text(i, v + 0.001, f'{v:.3f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
ax1.legend()

ax2.bar(labels, flops, color=[C['ok'], C['fail']], edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax2.set_ylabel('TDLC FLOPs (% of dense large)')
ax2.set_title('FLOPs (2-expert uses 9 pp MORE)')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 80)
for i, v in enumerate(flops):
    ax2.text(i, v + 1, f'{v}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('ABLATION: 3 experts > 2 experts. Nano absorbs hopeless low-SNR slots.',
             fontsize=12, y=1.02, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

5.3 Does explicit SNR knowledge help? (exp38, today)¶

The router currently uses pooled stem features — implicit channel quality. We hypothesized that adding explicit domain statistics (received power, channel power, channel variance — all SNR proxies computable at inference) might help.

Result: complete collapse to 100% large.

This is actually a clean finding: the implicit stem features already encode what those statistics capture. Explicitly handing the router an SNR proxy made it too easy to identify "large is always best" and lock in.

Interpretation: the channel-aware routing is already leveraging domain knowledge — just learned, not hand-engineered.

In [10]:
# exp38 SNR-input ablation
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 5))

labels = ['exp26\n(implicit features)', 'exp38\n(+ explicit SNR proxies)']
bler = [0.902, 0.897]
flops = [56, 100]

ax1.bar(labels, bler, color=[C['ok'], C['fail']], edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax1.axhline(y=0.901, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Dense large')
ax1.set_ylabel('Average BLER')
ax1.set_title('BLER — similar (both ~ dense large)')
ax1.set_ylim(0.88, 0.93)
for i, v in enumerate(bler):
    ax1.text(i, v + 0.001, f'{v:.3f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
ax1.legend()

ax2.bar(labels, flops, color=[C['ok'], C['fail']], edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax2.set_ylabel('FLOPs (% of dense large)')
ax2.set_title('FLOPs — exp38 collapsed to 100% large')
ax2.set_ylim(0, 120)
for i, v in enumerate(flops):
    ax2.text(i, v + 2, f'{v}%', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

plt.suptitle('ABLATION: Explicit SNR proxies cause Phase-2 collapse. Implicit features are sufficient.',
             fontsize=12, y=1.02, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

6. Honest weaknesses — what we own¶

6.1 Seed stability — bimodal¶

We re-ran α=2e-3 with two additional seeds (32 and 42) alongside our headline seed 67.

In [11]:
# 3-seed bimodal result
seeds = pd.DataFrame({
    'Seed': ['s67 (exp26)', 's42 (exp29)', 's32 (exp28)'],
    'BLER': [0.902, 0.902, 0.958],
    'Routing l/n/s': ['44/15/40', '55/12/33', '0/49/51'],
    'Outcome': ['Heterogeneous ✓', 'Heterogeneous ✓', 'Large COLLAPSED ✗'],
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.5))
colors_s = [C['ok'], C['ok'], C['fail']]
ax.bar(seeds['Seed'], seeds['BLER'], color=colors_s, edgecolor='black', linewidth=0.9)
ax.axhline(y=0.901, color=C['large'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.2, label='Dense large (0.901)')
ax.set_ylabel('Average BLER')
ax.set_title('Seed stability: 2 of 3 reproduce, 1 collapses to a 5pp-worse attractor')
ax.set_ylim(0.85, 1.0)
for i, (v, o) in enumerate(zip(seeds['BLER'], seeds['Outcome'])):
    ax.text(i, v + 0.005, f'{v:.3f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
    ax.text(i, 0.86, o, ha='center', fontsize=9.5)
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

display(seeds)

print("\nBoth stabilization recipes we tried — large-warmup and β-warmup — FAILED:")
print("  • large-warmup (freeze nano+small for 2k steps): 3/3 seeds → 100% large (over-corrects)")
print("  • β-warmup (β=0.5 for 4k steps, drop to 0.1):    mean BLER 0.938 ± 0.024 (worse than baseline)")
print("\nWe report the bimodal distribution honestly. Recipe stability is an open problem.")
Seed BLER Routing l/n/s Outcome
0 s67 (exp26) 0.902 44/15/40 Heterogeneous ✓
1 s42 (exp29) 0.902 55/12/33 Heterogeneous ✓
2 s32 (exp28) 0.958 0/49/51 Large COLLAPSED ✗ 
Both stabilization recipes we tried — large-warmup and β-warmup — FAILED:
  • large-warmup (freeze nano+small for 2k steps): 3/3 seeds → 100% large (over-corrects)
  • β-warmup (β=0.5 for 4k steps, drop to 0.1):    mean BLER 0.938 ± 0.024 (worse than baseline)

We report the bimodal distribution honestly. Recipe stability is an open problem.

6.2 OOD generalization — synthetic-only scope¶

We tested on DeepMIMO ray-traced data (ASU campus, 3.5 GHz) — real propagation, not 3GPP stochastic models.

All three of our models — including dense large — fail catastrophically (~0.99 BLER).

We then tried a few-shot fine-tune (500 steps, 2k OOD samples).

In [12]:
ood = pd.DataFrame({
    'Model': ['dense_large', 'exp26 (3-expert)', 'exp31 (2-expert)'],
    'TDLC BLER (in-dist)': [0.866, 0.867, 0.878],
    'OOD BLER (zero-shot)': [0.990, 0.992, 0.993],
    'OOD BLER (post-FT)': [0.9901, 0.9915, '—'],
    'OOD FLOPs %': [100, 32, 71],
})

display(ood.style.set_caption('DeepMIMO OOD evaluation — all models fail; brief fine-tune insufficient'))

print("\nKey findings:")
print("  ✗ Synthetic-trained NRX cannot decode ray-traced channels without OOD adaptation.")
print("  ✗ 500 steps / 2k samples is INSUFFICIENT to bridge the synthetic-vs-ray-traced gap.")
print("  ✓ NO catastrophic forgetting on in-distribution data.")
print("  ✓ Routing did NOT adapt to OOD (nano-default behavior preserved).")
print("  ⚪ Bonus: 3-expert MoE *uses less compute* on OOD (router defaults to nano under unfamiliar features).")
print("\nHonest scope statement: this work is for synthetic 3GPP channels.")
print("Bridging to ray-traced needs more data and training time than we had — out of scope.")
DeepMIMO OOD evaluation — all models fail; brief fine-tune insufficient
  Model TDLC BLER (in-dist) OOD BLER (zero-shot) OOD BLER (post-FT) OOD FLOPs %
0 dense_large 0.866000 0.990000 0.990100 100
1 exp26 (3-expert) 0.867000 0.992000 0.991500 32
2 exp31 (2-expert) 0.878000 0.993000 — 71 
Key findings:
  ✗ Synthetic-trained NRX cannot decode ray-traced channels without OOD adaptation.
  ✗ 500 steps / 2k samples is INSUFFICIENT to bridge the synthetic-vs-ray-traced gap.
  ✓ NO catastrophic forgetting on in-distribution data.
  ✓ Routing did NOT adapt to OOD (nano-default behavior preserved).
  ⚪ Bonus: 3-expert MoE *uses less compute* on OOD (router defaults to nano under unfamiliar features).

Honest scope statement: this work is for synthetic 3GPP channels.
Bridging to ray-traced needs more data and training time than we had — out of scope.

6.3 SNR-oracle baseline — theoretical upper bound¶

We built a hand-rule cascade (NOT a trained model) that uses true SNR to pick the cheapest expert that clears a BLER tolerance. This shows the theoretical ceiling of what's achievable with these three experts.

In [13]:
oracle = pd.DataFrame({
    'Strategy': ['Per-profile static (best fixed rule)',
                 'SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.01..0.02',
                 'exp26 LEARNED MoE',
                 'SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.05',
                 'SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.10'],
    'Avg BLER': [0.900, 0.900, 0.902, 0.909, 0.919],
    'FLOPs %': [100, 49, 56, 35, 27],
    'Notes': ['both -> large always', 'oracle SNR access (NOT deployable)',
              'NO oracle access', 'aggressive: relax BLER', 'very aggressive']
})

def style_oracle(s):
    return ['background-color: #fff4d6' if 'exp26' in r else '' for r in s]

display(oracle.style.apply(style_oracle, subset=['Strategy'])
        .set_caption('SNR-oracle cascade -- theoretical baseline (true SNR not available at inference)'))

print()
print('Honest finding:')
print('  - exp26 (NO oracle access): 0.902 BLER at 56% FLOPs')
print('  - Oracle (with TRUE SNR):   0.900 BLER at 49% FLOPs')
print('  -> 7 pp FLOPs gap between learned routing and theoretical optimum.')
print()
print('Did we try closing the gap by feeding raw SNR proxies to the router?')
print('YES -- exp38 (next section). Result: 100% collapse to large.')
print('Implicit stem features outperformed explicit raw signal statistics.')
print()
print('A properly trained SNR-estimator MODULE (not raw stats) might behave')
print('differently -- that is the genuinely open future direction.')
SNR-oracle cascade -- theoretical baseline (true SNR not available at inference)
  Strategy Avg BLER FLOPs % Notes
0 Per-profile static (best fixed rule) 0.900000 100 both -> large always
1 SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.01..0.02 0.900000 49 oracle SNR access (NOT deployable)
2 exp26 LEARNED MoE 0.902000 56 NO oracle access
3 SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.05 0.909000 35 aggressive: relax BLER
4 SNR-oracle cascade tol+0.10 0.919000 27 very aggressive 
Honest finding:
  - exp26 (NO oracle access): 0.902 BLER at 56% FLOPs
  - Oracle (with TRUE SNR):   0.900 BLER at 49% FLOPs
  -> 7 pp FLOPs gap between learned routing and theoretical optimum.

Did we try closing the gap by feeding raw SNR proxies to the router?
YES -- exp38 (next section). Result: 100% collapse to large.
Implicit stem features outperformed explicit raw signal statistics.

A properly trained SNR-estimator MODULE (not raw stats) might behave
differently -- that is the genuinely open future direction.

7. Per-SNR routing — the router learned a difficulty-dependent policy¶

Decomposing expert usage by SNR bin reveals the router has learned a difficulty-dependent routing policy within each channel profile, not just per-profile preferences.

In [14]:
# Show per-SNR routing if available
for figname, caption in [
    ('expert_usage_by_snr.png', 'Expert routing by SNR bin (test split)'),
    ('bler_by_snr_comparison.png', 'BLER comparison across SNR bins'),
    ('waterfall_dense_baselines.png', 'TDLC waterfall region — dense baselines'),
]:
    if fig_exists(figname):
        print(f'\n{caption}:')
        display(Image(filename=str(FIGURES / figname), width=850))

Expert routing by SNR bin (test split):

BLER comparison across SNR bins:

TDLC waterfall region — dense baselines:

Interpretation of the per-SNR routing:

  • On TDLC, at low SNR (-8 dB) the router uses 84% small + 16% nano (large is never used) — at this SNR all experts fail with BLER=1.0, so the router saves compute by routing cheap.
  • In the waterfall region (5–9 dB), the router transitions toward large where capacity actually reduces errors.
  • At high SNR (18 dB on TDLC), 93% of traffic goes to large.
  • On UMA, nano dominates at low SNR with a gradual shift toward large at high SNR.

The router does NOT follow a naive "hard → large, easy → nano" policy. It learned to save compute when no expert can succeed, and only spend it when capacity matters.

8. What we did vs. prior work¶

Work What they did What we add
Wiesmayr et al. 2024 Defines dense NRX architecture We use it as the baseline
MEAN (van Bolderik 2024) MoE for 5G NRX, homogeneous experts, per-SNR specialisation, CDL-C only We use heterogeneous experts (different compute), FLOPs penalty, mixed UMa+TDLC, full Pareto analysis. Orthogonal contribution: they target robustness, we target compute efficiency.
Song et al. 2025 Channel-aware gating in wireless We instantiate this on top of an NRX with an explicit FLOPs penalty in the loss

Three things MEAN doesn't have:

  1. Heterogeneous expert sizes — actual compute heterogeneity, not just specialisation
  2. FLOPs penalty in the loss — emergent compute-aware routing
  3. Pareto analysis + ablations + oracle baseline — complete characterisation

9. Conclusions — what's real, what isn't¶

What works ✅¶

  • exp26 routes adaptively. It sends easy channels (high SNR, UMa) to cheap experts and hard channels (waterfall TDLC) to large — without being told SNR.
  • 56% FLOPs at matching BLER is real. Hardware-agnostic, relevant for ASIC/FPGA/energy.
  • Channel-aware features are causal (random-router ablation: 6.6 pp BLER drop).
  • 3 experts > 2 experts (drop-nano ablation: 0.7 pp BLER + 9 pp FLOPs cost).
  • Asym warm-start is the only recipe that works — Phase 1 and Phase 2 both fail in opposite directions, characterized cleanly.
  • Implicit features are sufficient (exp38 with explicit SNR proxies collapses).

Honest limitations ⚠️¶

  • Seed stability is bimodal: 2/3 seeds reproduce, 1 collapses. Two stabilization recipes both failed.
  • OOD on ray-traced data fails — synthetic-only scope. Brief fine-tune insufficient.
  • Slight gap to oracle: with true SNR a hand-rule cascade reaches 49% FLOPs; we reach 56%. We tried closing it with raw signal stats (exp38) — that collapsed.

Future work — three concrete experiments before the final deadline¶

  1. Proper inference latency benchmark with efficient dispatch. Implement production-grade sparse-MoE dispatch (cf. Mixtral, vLLM kernels) and re-benchmark exp26 vs dense_large at multiple batch sizes. Current naive sequential dispatch doesn't translate FLOPs savings into wall-clock — we need to verify whether efficient dispatch fixes this.
  2. High-resolution per-SNR evaluation. Current 7 bins per profile leaves only 2 points in the TDLC waterfall (14 dB, 18 dB). Resample at 1-2 dB steps across 10-20 dB to precisely characterise where exp26 diverges from dense_large.
  3. Router interpretability — what does the router see? PCA/UMAP of stem features coloured by selected expert and by true SNR; saliency maps over the input grid; per-expert feature activation analysis. Preliminary PCA exists on the cluster — needs to be deepened.

Beyond these three, longer-horizon open problems: seed-stable training recipe; domain-randomized pretraining for OOD.

10. One number per claim (for quick reference)¶

Claim Evidence
Compute-aware MoE works exp26: 0.902 BLER at 56% FLOPs (vs dense_large 0.901 at 100%)
Router uses channel features random-router ablation: BLER craters 6.6 pp
3 experts > 2 experts drop-nano: 0.7 pp BLER + 9 pp FLOPs cost
Asym warm-start is the only recipe Phase 1 and Phase 2 both fail; 5 anti-collapse mechanisms also failed
Implicit features are sufficient exp38 (explicit SNR) collapses to 100% large
Seed stability 2/3 seeds reproduce, 1 collapses; bimodal distribution
OOD generalization synthetic-only scope; few-shot fine-tune insufficient
Oracle gap hand-rule with true SNR reaches 49% FLOPs vs our 56%

Status: experiments essentially complete (~10 distinct experiments, all with results). Remaining work: LaTeX rewrite of checkpoint report, poster (deadline 13 days).
